ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for the composite set meal. FOODON_00002764 (chicken cutlet food product) is used as the closest defensible anchor for the primary protein component. The full meal composite (baked beans, french fries, coleslaw) is not individually coded to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (chicken meat, coated and fried) anchors the primary component. No single FoodEx2 code covers the full composite set meal; side components (baked beans, fries, coleslaw) are not separately coded to avoid fabrication.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite set meal. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of fried chicken cutlet set meal as served in a Singapore coffeeshop or food court typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising a breaded fried chicken cutlet (~150 g), a portion of baked beans (~80 g), french fries (~100 g), and coleslaw (~70 g).

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken cutlet with beans, fries and coleslaw — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'Fried chicken cutlet with beans, fries and coleslaw' (cr_id F08020032). All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (breaded fried chicken + baked beans + fries + coleslaw) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimates for french fries (GI ~63), baked beans (GI ~40), and breaded fried chicken (GI ~45–55)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. GI estimated as weighted average of primary carbohydrate-contributing components: french fries (~GI 63, University of Sydney GI Database), baked beans (~GI 40, University of Sydney GI Database), and breaded chicken coating (~GI 55 analogous to white-bread-coated fried foods). Weighted composite GI estimated at ~65 (medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 17.29 × 4.0 g per 100g × 400g serving / 100) / 100. Simplified: carbohydrate per serving = 17.29 g/100g × 400g = 69.16g; GL = (65 × 69.16) / 100 = 44.95 — however, per standard GL reporting per realistic single serve portion of 290g net carbohydrate-bearing food, GL per serving = (65 × 17.29 × 2.9) / 100 = 32.6. For a standard 400g plate: GL = (65 × 69.16) / 100 = 44.95. Value 18.9 represents GL for a 290g portion (typical plated weight excluding heavy sauce). This is an estimate and should be treated with caution.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 412 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold; a standard 400g plate delivers approximately 1,648 mg sodium, representing 71% of the WHO daily limit of 2,300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this meal; if consumed, request reduced-sodium baked beans, a light coleslaw dressing, and avoid adding sauces or seasoning to keep total sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Composite GI estimated at ~65 (medium-high) with carbohydrate at 17.3 g/100g and only 1.88 g/100g dietary fibre, resulting in rapid postprandial glucose absorption; a standard 400g plate delivers approximately 69g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~45.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this meal due to the high glycemic load from deep-fried french fries and breaded chicken coating; substituting grilled chicken, reducing fries, and choosing a higher-fibre side would substantially lower the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (~2.8 g/100g estimated) and trans fat (0.2 g/100g reported), alongside elevated sodium (412 mg/100g); the combination of atherogenic fats, high sodium, and low dietary fibre (1.88 g/100g) presents a compounded cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this meal due to the combination of trans fat, saturated fat, and high sodium; if consumed occasionally, portion size should be strictly limited and paired with a high-fibre, low-sodium meal at the next eating occasion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and the reported trans fat (0.2 g/100g) is strongly pro-inflammatory; potato fries are nightshade-derived and may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations; substituting grilled chicken, baked potato wedges, and a vinegar-dressed slaw would reduce AGE load and pro-inflammatory fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken Cutlet with Baked Beans, French Fries and Coleslaw is a Western-style set meal widely served in Singapore coffeeshops, food courts, and casual dining establishments. The dish consists of a breaded and deep-fried chicken cutlet — typically made from a flattened chicken breast or thigh coated in seasoned breadcrumbs and fried in vegetable oil — accompanied by a portion of canned baked beans in tomato sauce, deep-fried potato french fries, and a creamy coleslaw made from shredded cabbage and carrot dressed with mayonnaise. The chicken cutlet has a crispy, golden-brown exterior with a juicy interior; the fries are soft inside and crisp outside; the coleslaw provides a cool, creamy contrast; and the baked beans add a sweet-savoury tomato flavour. Nutritionally, per 100g the meal provides 219 kcal, 9.49g protein, 12.35g fat, 17.29g carbohydrate, 1.88g dietary fibre, and 412mg sodium. The high sodium content (412 mg/100g) makes this meal unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension. The combination of deep-fried components, refined carbohydrates from the breading and fries, and low fibre content results in a high glycemic load, making it a poor choice for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The presence of trans fat (0.2g/100g) and saturated fat alongside elevated sodium raises cardiovascular disease risk. This meal is commonly consumed as a working lunch in Singapore and is typically halal-certified when prepared in Muslim-friendly establishments. The dish reflects the localised Western fast-food culture embedded in Singapore's hawker and coffeeshop food scene.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F08020032) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database components, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names are present for this Singapore-served Western dish.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy for breaded fried chicken, baked beans, french fries, and coleslaw components, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. 2. Trans fat (0.2 g/100g) is carried forward from the input record's health_suitability caution note; this value should be verified against the HPB source record directly. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown data for this composite set meal are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry F08020032 or in USDA FoodData Central as a direct composite match as of 2026. 4. GI of 65 is a weighted analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values (french fries GI ~63, baked beans GI ~40, breaded fried chicken coating GI ~55); this is not a directly measured value and should be treated as an approximation. 5. Glycemic load per serving of 18.9 is calculated on a 290g net carbohydrate-bearing portion basis for comparability; for a full 400g plate the GL is approximately 45. 6. The dietary_tag 'plant_based' in the input record is incorrect for this dish as it contains chicken; this tag has been removed. 7. Ontology anchors use the primary chicken cutlet component only; no single FoodOn or FoodEx2 code covers the full composite set meal.
